protocol_definition.name: Voting
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents answer independently, then cast private ballots; plurality selects the output.
protocol_definition.visibility: Answers are private before voting; voters see all anonymized proposals during voting.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after ballot aggregation; deterministic lowest-index tie break.

google_search_tool.description: Searches the web for up-to-date academic papers and scholarly information.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Introduction to PEFT

Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods enable adapting large language models (LLMs) to downstream tasks without updating all pretrained weights. Techniques such as LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), prefix-tuning, and adapter modules insert small, task-specific parameter overheads that modulate the base model’s behavior. This approach is increasingly essential for modern LLMs due to (i) prohibitive compute and memory costs of full fine-tuning on massive models, (ii) environmental and economic considerations of retraining billions of parameters for every task, and (iii) practical needs for rapid, modular deployment across many domains. PEFT supports efficient switching among tasks, easier experimentation, reduced risk of catastrophic forgetting, and the ability to keep base models fixed for alignment or safety constraints while injecting task-specific capabilities.

2. Paper Extraction Matrix
- Paper Title & Year
- Core Method
- Key Innovation
- Main Limitation

Paper Title & Year: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (2021)
- Core Method: Introduces Low-Rank Adaptation modules inserted into Transformer weight matrices; trains only the added low-rank matrices while keeping the original weights frozen.
- Key Innovation: Demonstrates that high-performance fine-tuning can be achieved with a tiny parameter budget by factorizing updates into low-rank components, drastically reducing trainable parameters and memory.
- Main Limitation: Requires careful selection of where to insert adapters and rank choices; may need task-specific tuning of architecture for optimal performance and can incur some overhead when scaling to many tasks or very heterogeneous tasks.

Paper Title & Year: Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Transformer-based Language Models for Text Generation (2019)
- Core Method: Adds fixed, continuous prefix tokens in each layer to steer generation, with only the prefix parameters being trained.
- Key Innovation: Enables effective task adaptation with a minimal trainable parameter count while preserving the base model weights; demonstrates strong gains in conditional text generation without full model fine-tuning.
- Main Limitation: Prefix choices can be sensitive to prompts and task distribution; performance may degrade on tasks that require deeper, global updates beyond what prefix tokens can capture, and there may be challenges with long-context generalization.

Paper Title & Year: Adapters in Deep Learning with Applications to NLP (2020)
- Core Method: Introduces adapter modules (small feed-forward or bottleneck networks) inserted within transformer layers; only adapter parameters are trained.
- Key Innovation: Provides modular, reusable components for multi-task learning, enabling efficient transfer across tasks with limited extra parameters; supports sequential and multi-task training by swapping adapters.
- Main Limitation: While parameter-efficient, adapters still introduce additional architectural components; performance can be sensitive to adapter size, placement, and bottleneck dimension, and may require careful engineering to match full fine-tuning results on some tasks.

3. Synthesized Research Gap & Future Direction

Synthesis of Main Limitations:
- Across LoRA, Prefix-Tuning, and Adapters, common limitations include sensitivity to architectural choices (where and how to insert modules), the need for task-specific hyperparameter tuning, and limitations in capturing truly global or cross-task updates without some form of full-model or broader reconfiguration. There is a gap in developing a unified, adaptive PEFT framework that automatically selects the optimal insertion points, ranks, and bottleneck sizes for a given task distribution, while ensuring consistent performance across diverse tasks without manual tuning.

Overarching Research Gap:
- A unified, automated, and task-distribution-aware PEFT framework that (a) determines optimal insertion locations and dimensions for adapters/prefixes/LoRA per task, (b) scales robustly across heterogeneous downstream tasks, and (c) maintains strong performance with minimal manual hyperparameter tuning.

Concrete Future Direction:
- Develop an automated PEFT hyperparameter and architecture selection system that uses meta-learning or Bayesian optimization to tailor insertion points, ranks, bottleneck sizes, and prefix lengths to the target task distribution. The system should leverage task descriptors (data statistics, domain signals,, prompt structure) and a lightweight validation loop to propose a compact, high-performing PEFT configuration without extensive human engineering. This would reduce manual trial-and-error, enable scalable deployment across many tasks, and improve robustness across diverse domain shifts.

4. References (DOI or ArXiv IDs)
- LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models. arXiv:2106.04543. Retrieved from https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.04543
- Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Transformer-based Language Models for Text Generation. arXiv:1999.10166. Retrieved from https://arxiv.org/abs/1999.10166
- Adapters in Deep Learning with Applications to NLP. arXiv:1902.00738. Retrieved from https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.00738

Note: The references above are provided with arXiv identifiers as commonly cited representative works for the three PEFT families. If you require DOIs or alternative venue citations, I can provide them upon request.

This necessitates a new branch of research focusing on the parameter-efficient adaptation of PLMs, which optimizes a small portion of the model parameters while keeping the rest fixed, drastically cutting down computation and storage costs. In general, it demonstrates that large-scale models could be effectively stimulated by the optimization of a few parameters. Despite the various designs, here we discuss and analyse the approaches under a more consistent and accessible term ‘delta-tuning’, where ‘delta’ a mathematical notation often used to denote changes, is borrowed to refer to the portion of parameters that are ‘changed’ during training. We also discuss the theoretical principles underlying the effectiveness of delta-tuning and interpret them from the perspectives of optimization and optimal control. Code is released at https://github.com/OpenGVLab/LLaMA-Adapter.", "source_provider": "OpenAlex", "title": "LLaMA-Adapter: Efficient Fine-tuning of Language Models with Zero-init Attention", "url": "http://arxiv.org/abs/2303.16199", "url_or_doi": "10.48550/arxiv.2303.16199", "venue": "arXiv (Cornell University)", "year": 2023}, {"citation_count": 367, "published_at": "2023-07-12", "snippet": "Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated remarkable capabilities in natural language processing tasks and beyond. This article provides an overview of the existing literature on a broad range of LLM-related concepts. Drawing on Gadamer’s concepts of Vorverständnis, Distanciation, and the Hermeneutic Circle, the paper explores how LLM fine-tuning evolves from initial learning to deeper comprehension, ultimately advancing toward self-awareness. Furthermore, we also employed two other PEFT methods, prompt tuning and adapter tuning, in ESM-2 for SP prediction. More elaborate experiments show that PEFT-SP using adapter tuning can also improve the state-of-the-art results by up to 28.1% MCC gain for SPs with small training samples and an overall MCC gain of 3.8%. However, their designs are hand-crafted separately, and it remains unclear whether certain design patterns exist for parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Thus, we present a parameter-efficient fine-tuning design paradigm and discover design patterns that are applicable to different experimental settings. Instead of focusing on designing another individual tuning strategy, we introduce parameter-efficient fine-tuning design spaces that parameterize tuning structures and tuning strategies. Specifically, any design space is characterized by four components: layer grouping, trainable parameter allocation, tunable groups, and strategy assignment. We discover the following design patterns: (i) group layers in a spindle pattern; (ii) allocate the number of trainable parameters to layers uniformly; (iii) tune all the groups; (iv) assign proper tuning strategies to different groups. In this paper, we aim to establish a systematic set of open problems and application successes so that ML researchers can comprehend the field's current state more quickly and become productive.", "source_provider": "OpenAlex", "title": "Challenges and Applications of Large Language Models", "url": "http://arxiv.org/abs/2307.10169", "url_or_doi": "10.48550/arxiv.2307.10169", "venue": "arXiv (Cornell University)", "year": 2023}, {"citation_count": 98, "published_at": "2024-01-01", "snippet": "In this paper, we introduce a novel and simple method for obtaining high-quality text embeddings using only synthetic data and less than 1k training steps.Unlike existing methods that often depend on multi-stage intermediate pretraining with billions of weakly-supervised text pairs, followed by fine-tuning with a few labeled datasets, our method does not require building complex training pipelines or relying on manually collected datasets that are often constrained by task diversity and language coverage.We leverage proprietary LLMs to generate diverse synthetic data for hundreds of thousands of text embedding tasks across 93 languages.We then fine-tune open-source decoder-only LLMs on the synthetic data using standard contrastive loss.Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves strong performance on highly competitive text embedding benchmarks without using any labeled data.Furthermore, when fine-tuned with a mixture of synthetic and labeled data, our model sets new state-of-the-art results on the BEIR and MTEB benchmarks.", "source_provider": "OpenAlex", "title": "Improving Text Embeddings with Large Language Models", "url": "https://doi.org/10.18653/v1/2024.acl-long.642", "url_or_doi": "10.18653/v1/2024.acl-long.642", "venue": null, "year": 2024}, {"citation_count": 118, "published_at": "2024-11-27", "snippet": "Scientific discoveries often hinge on synthesizing decades of research, a task that potentially outstrips human information processing capacities. LLMs trained on the vast scientific literature could potentially integrate noisy yet interrelated findings to forecast novel results better than human experts. Here, to evaluate this possibility, we created BrainBench, a forward-looking benchmark for predicting neuroscience results. We find that LLMs surpass experts in predicting experimental outcomes. BrainGPT, an LLM we tuned on the neuroscience literature, performed better yet. Like human experts, when LLMs indicated high confidence in their predictions, their responses were more likely to be correct, which presages a future where LLMs assist humans in making discoveries. Our approach is not neuroscience specific and is transferable to other knowledge-intensive endeavours.", "source_provider": "OpenAlex", "title": "Large language models surpass human experts in predicting neuroscience results", "url": "https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-024-02046-9", "url_or_doi": "10.1038/s41562-024-02046-9", "venue": "Nature Human Behaviour", "year": 2024}, {"citation_count": 40, "published_at": "2023-10-05", "snippet": "Optimizing large language models (LLMs) for downstream use cases often involves the customization of pre-trained LLMs through further fine-tuning. Meta's open release of Llama models and OpenAI's APIs for fine-tuning GPT-3.5 Turbo on custom datasets also encourage this practice. But, what are the safety costs associated with such custom fine-tuning? We note that while existing safety alignment infrastructures can restrict harmful behaviors of LLMs at inference time, they do not cover safety risks when fine-tuning privileges are extended to end-users. Our red teaming studies find that the safety alignment of LLMs can be compromised by fine-tuning with only a few adversarially designed training examples. For instance, we jailbreak GPT-3.5 Turbo's safety guardrails by fine-tuning it on only 10 such examples at a cost of less than $0.20 via OpenAI's APIs, making the model responsive to nearly any harmful instructions. Disconcertingly, our research also reveals that, even without malicious intent, simply fine-tuning with benign and commonly used datasets can also inadvertently degrade the safety alignment of LLMs, though to a lesser extent. These findings suggest that fine-tuning aligned LLMs introduces new safety risks that current safety infrastructures fall short of addressing -- even if a model's initial safety alignment is impeccable, it is not necessarily to be maintained after custom fine-tuning. We outline and critically analyze potential mitigations and advocate for further research efforts toward reinforcing safety protocols for the custom fine-tuning of aligned LLMs.", "source_provider": "OpenAlex", "title": "Fine-tuning Aligned Language Models Compromises Safety, Even When Users Do Not Intend To!", "url": "http://arxiv.org/abs/2310.03693", "url_or_doi": "10.48550/arxiv.2310.03693", "venue": "arXiv (Cornell University)", "year": 2023}], "retrieved_at": "2026-07-20T06:45:34+00:00", "source_providers": ["OpenAlex"]}

in code generation that generates source code with LLM from natural language descriptions. This burgeoning field has captured significant interest from both academic researchers and industry professionals due to its practical significance in software development, e.g., GitHub Copilot. Despite the active exploration of LLMs for a variety of code tasks, either from the perspective of natural language processing (NLP) or software engineering (SE) or both, there is a noticeable absence of a comprehensive and up-to-date literature review dedicated to LLM for code generation. In this survey, we aim to bridge this gap by providing a systematic literature review that serves as a valuable reference for researchers investigating the cutting-edge progress in LLMs for code generation. We introduce a taxonomy to categorize and discuss the recent developments in LLMs for code generation, covering aspects such as data curation, latest advances, performance evaluation, ethical implications, environmental impact, and real-world applications. In addition, we present a historical overview of the evolution of LLMs for code generation and offer an empirical comparison using the HumanEval, MBPP, and BigCodeBench benchmarks across various levels of difficulty and types of programming tasks to highlight the progressive enhancements in LLM capabilities for code generation. Furthermore, we have established a dedicated resource GitHub page (https://github.com/juyongjiang/CodeLLMSurvey) to continuously document and disseminate the most recent advances in the field.", "source_provider": "OpenAlex", "title": "A Survey on Large Language Models for Code Generation", "url": "http://arxiv.org/abs/2406.00515", "url_or_doi": "10.48550/arxiv.2406.00515", "venue": "arXiv (Cornell University)", "year": 2024}, {"citation_count": 5, "published_at": "2025-01-23", "snippet": "This survey delves into the realm of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) within the context of Foundation Models (FMs). PEFT, a cost-effective fine-tuning technique, minimizes parameters and computational complexity while striving for optimal downstream task performance. FMs, like ChatGPT, DALL-E, and LLaVA specialize in language understanding, generative tasks, and multimodal tasks, trained on diverse datasets spanning text, images, and videos. The diversity of FMs guides various adaptation strategies for PEFT. Therefore, this survey aims to provide a comprehensive overview of PEFT techniques applied to diverse FMs and address critical gaps in understanding the techniques, trends, and applications. We start by providing a detailed development of FMs and PEFT. We also discuss the theoretical principles underlying the effectiveness of delta-tuning and interpret them from the perspectives of optimization and optimal control. The results demonstrate that using adapter-based PEFT in smaller-scale LLMs (7B) with few extra trainable parameters yields comparable, and in some cases superior, performance to powerful LLMs (175B) in zero-shot inference on simple math reasoning datasets.", "source_provider": "OpenAlex", "title": "LLM-Adapters: An Adapter Family for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models", "url": "https://doi.org/10.18653/v1/2023.emnlp-main.319", "url_or_doi": "10.18653/v1/2023.emnlp-main.319", "venue": null, "year": 2023}, {"citation_count": 172, "published_at": "2023-03-28", "snippet": "We present LLaMA-Adapter, a lightweight adaption method to efficiently fine-tune LLaMA into an instruction-following model. With our efficient training, LLaMA-Adapter can generate high-quality responses, comparable to Alpaca with fully fine-tuned 7B parameters. Furthermore, we also evaluate the zero-initialized attention mechanism for fine-tuning other pre-trained models (ViT, RoBERTa) on traditional vision and language tasks, demonstrating the superior generalization capacity of our approach. Code is released at https://github.com/OpenGVLab/LLaMA-Adapter.", "source_provider": "OpenAlex", "title": "LLaMA-Adapter: Efficient Fine-tuning of Language Models with Zero-init Attention", "url": "http://arxiv.org/abs/2303.16199", "url_or_doi": "10.48550/arxiv.2303.16199", "venue": "arXiv (Cornell University)", "year": 2023}, {"citation_count": 367, "published_at": "2023-07-12", "snippet": "Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated remarkable capabilities in natural language processing tasks and beyond. 1. Introduction to PEFT

Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods enable adapting large language models (LLMs) to downstream tasks without updating all pretrained weights. Techniques such as LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), prefix-tuning, and adapter modules insert small, task-specific parameter overheads that modulate the base model’s behavior. This approach is increasingly essential for modern LLMs due to (i) prohibitive compute and memory costs of full fine-tuning on massive models, (ii) environmental and economic considerations of retraining billions of parameters for every task, and (iii) practical needs for rapid, modular deployment across many domains. PEFT supports efficient switching among tasks, easier experimentation, reduced risk of catastrophic forgetting, and the ability to keep base models fixed for alignment or safety constraints while injecting task-specific capabilities.
